beats.prompt: Quick warm-up — match each clue to what made it the evidence.

pairs.right: his action, constrained by his rules, is the evidence

pairs.left: '≥1 boy' vs 'the older is a boy' (L1)

feedback.correct: The host's choice was the clue in L5; here the clue's exact wording is the whole update. Same lesson: name what you actually observed.
hints: From L5: the evidence was which door the host opened.
From L1: 'at least one boy' and 'the older is a boy' are different clues → different answers.
The exact event you condition on sets the number.

beats.prompt: A family has two children. I tell you at least one is a boy. What is P(both boys)?

options: 1/2 — the other child is a 50/50 coin flip
1/3 — list the families the clue allows

1/2 — the other child is a 50/50 coin fl.note: That secretly assumes you singled out one child. List {BB, BG, GB, GG}: '≥1 boy' keeps {BB, BG, GB} → BB is 1 of 3 → 1/3.

1/3 — list the families the clue allows.note: Right — '≥1 boy' keeps three equally-likely families and only one is both-boys → 1/3.

1/4 — both boys is rare.note: 1/4 is the prior, before the clue. 'At least one boy' rules out girl-girl, lifting it to 1/3.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll list the families.
The clue removes only girl-girl, not a whole child.
{BB, BG, GB} survive → 1 of 3 → 1/3.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on conditioning on an event — then continue.

interaction.title: Condition on an event, not a vibe
interaction.body: A clue is a subset of the sample space. Write the four equally-likely families {BB, BG, GB, GG}, then keep exactly the ones the clue allows. 'At least one boy' keeps {BB, BG, GB}; 'this specific child is a boy' keeps a different set. The answer is forced once you write the event down.

feedback.correct: You condition on an event — a written-down subset of outcomes — not a vibe about 'the other child.'
hints: Write the clue as a set of families, not a feeling.
'≥1 boy' is the subset {BB, BG, GB}; 'the older is a boy' is {BB, BG}.
The posterior is just the focal outcome's share of the surviving subset.

beats.prompt: Watch the prior split (1 of 4) become the conditioned posterior (1 of 3) — 'at least one boy' rules out girl-girl and lifts both-boys to 1/3.

interaction.evidence: at least one is a boy

hero.structuralReadout: Of 4 equally-likely families, 'at least one boy' keeps 3; only 1 is both-boys → 1/3.

feedback.correct: Of four equally-likely families, 'at least one boy' rules out only girl-girl — leaving three, of which one is both-boys. The bar settles at 1/3, not 1/2.
hints: Watch the prior (1 of 4) become the conditioned posterior.
The clue removes girl-girl, not a whole child.
Both-boys is 1 of the 3 surviving families → 1/3.

beats.prompt: Same two-child family, two different clues. Compute both.

fields.label: P(both boys | at least one is a boy)
P(both boys | this specific child is a boy)

feedback.correct: 'At least one is a boy' keeps {BB, BG, GB} → 1/3. 'This specific child is a boy' singles out one child → keeps {BB, BG} (or {BB, GB}) → 1/2. One word changes which outcomes survive.
hints: List {BB, BG, GB, GG} and cross out what each clue forbids.
'≥1 boy' removes only GG (3 left). 'This child is a boy' fixes one slot to B (2 left).

beats.prompt: Three cards: black-black (BB), white-white (WW), black-white (BW). You draw one and see a random face — it's black. What is P(the other side is also black)?

fields.label: P(other side black | you see a black face)

beats.interviewNote: Two-children and Bertrand's-box are classic conditioning traps (interview packs `two-children`, `showcase-bertrand-box`). The discipline that defeats both: enumerate the equally-likely atomic outcomes you actually observe (faces, not cards; ordered families, not 'the other child') before dividing.

feedback.correct: Count faces, not cards. Three equally-likely black faces (two on the black-black card, one on the mixed card); two of them have black on the back → 2/3. Seeing a face is stronger evidence for the all-black card.
hints: Three cards: black-black, white-white, black-mixed. You saw a black face — which faces could it be?
There are 3 equally-likely black faces, not 2 cards. Two of those faces sit on the black-black card.

beats.prompt: Match each clue to its posterior — four costumes, one principle.

pairs.left: At least one is a boy
This specific child is a boy
Bertrand: you see a black face
Monty: the host opens a goat door

feedback.correct: Four costumes, one principle: the update is whatever the conditioning event forces. Even Monty (L5) is the same move.
hints: Each clue is a different subset of outcomes.
Match by what the clue keeps: ≥1 boy → 3 families; this-child → 2; a black face → 3 faces; the host's forced door → 2 of 3.
≥1 boy → 1/3, this-child → 1/2, Bertrand → 2/3, Monty → 2/3.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 1/3 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Enumerate
lenses.body: {BB, BG, GB, GG}; '≥1 boy' keeps {BB, BG, GB}; BB is 1 of 3

lenses.label: Frequencies
lenses.body: Of 400 families: 100 BB, 100 BG, 100 GB, 100 GG; '≥1 boy' keeps 300; BB is 100 → 1/3

feedback.correct: Enumeration, the conditional formula, and a 400-family frequency count all give 1/3 — not a sophistry.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 1/3.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: a family has three children. I tell you at least one is a boy. What is P(all three are boys)?

interaction.scenario: A family has three children. I tell you at least one is a boy.

fields.label: \(P(\text{all three boys}\mid\text{at least one is a boy})\)

feedback.correct: Enumerate all \(2^3 = 8\) equally-likely three-child families: \[ \{BBB,\;BBG,\;BGB,\;GBB,\;BGG,\;GBG,\;GGB,\;GGG\} \] The clue \(\geq 1\text{ boy}\) removes only \(GGG\), leaving 7. Only \(BBB\) is all-boys: \[ P(\text{all boys}\mid\geq 1\text{ boy}) = \frac{P(\text{all boys})}{P(\geq 1\text{ boy})} = \frac{1/8}{7/8} = \frac{1}{7} \] The clue's form matches the two-child version, but the sample space is larger — recount rather than reuse \(\tfrac{1}{3}\).
hints: Three children means \(2^3 = 8\) equally-likely families — list them all, don't reuse the \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) from the two-child version.
The clue \(\geq 1\text{ boy}\) removes only \(GGG\), leaving 7 families; \(BBB\) is one of them.
\(P(\text{all boys}\mid\geq 1\text{ boy}) = \frac{1}{7}\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to consolidate the conditioning principle.

feedback.correct: The update is set by the exact event you condition on — write the clue as a subset of outcomes and the math is forced.
hints: 'At least one boy' → 1/3; 'this child is a boy' → 1/2. Same family, different clue, different answer.
Bertrand: count faces, not cards → 2/3. Monty: the forced door → 2/3.
Next up: which way does the conditional point — P(E|H) vs P(H|E).
